children.name: Caecilia
children.description: Large burrowing caecilians.

children.name: Caecilia thompsoni
children.description: The longest caecilian at up to 1.5 m; powerful burrower of Colombian rainforest; hundreds of annuli (body rings).

children.name: Caecilia tentaculata
children.description: South American tropical forests.

children.name: Caecilia nigricans
children.description: A large dark caecilian of Colombia and Ecuador, reaching over a meter in length. A powerful soil burrower of lowland and foothill forests.

children.name: Caecilia orozcoi
children.description: A Colombian caecilian known from Andean foothill forests, distinguished by its annulation pattern. Poorly known and rarely encountered.

children.name: Caecilia subnigricans
children.description: A burrowing caecilian of northern South American forests, similar to but smaller than the black caecilian, with a darkish body.

children.name: Caecilia occidentalis
children.description: A burrowing caecilian of the Pacific lowlands of Colombia and Ecuador, inhabiting moist forest soils.

children.name: Typhlonectes
children.description: Fully aquatic caecilians.

children.name: Typhlonectes compressicauda
children.description: Fully aquatic; laterally compressed tail for swimming; bears live young; widely kept in aquaria and popular in research on limbless locomotion.

children.name: Typhlonectes natans
children.description: Colombian river systems.

children.name: Typhlonectes cunhai
children.description: An aquatic caecilian of South American rivers and swamps, fully adapted to life in water with a finned tail region. Gives live birth to larvae provisioned in the oviduct.

children.name: Ichthyophis
children.description: Asian caecilians with parental care.

children.name: Ichthyophis glutinosus
children.description: Lays eggs in moist burrows; female coils around clutch for months until hatching; one of the most studied oviparous caecilians.

children.name: Ichthyophis bombayensis
children.description: Western Ghats of India.

children.name: Ichthyophis kohtaoensis
children.description: A striped caecilian of Thailand, Laos and Cambodia, often found in rice paddies and stream banks. Females coil around their eggs until they hatch into aquatic larvae.

children.name: Ichthyophis bannanicus
children.description: The only caecilian native to China, found in Yunnan and Guangxi as well as Vietnam. A striped, egg-laying caecilian of moist lowland habitats.

children.name: Ichthyophis beddomei
children.description: A striped caecilian of the Western Ghats of India, inhabiting moist forest soils and plantations. Females guard their egg clutches.

children.name: Ichthyophis tricolor
children.description: A striped caecilian endemic to the southern Western Ghats of India, named for its tricolored appearance. Lives in moist soil and leaf litter.

children.name: Ichthyophis malabarensis
children.description: A caecilian of the Malabar region of the Western Ghats, India. A soil-dwelling, egg-laying species of forested hill habitats.

children.name: Ichthyophis orthoplicatus
children.description: A striped caecilian endemic to the wet zone and hills of Sri Lanka, found in moist soils of forests and tea plantations.

children.name: Ichthyophis humphreyi
children.description: A Southeast Asian striped caecilian of forest soils, an egg-laying species with aquatic larvae.

children.name: Siphonops
children.description: Skin-feeding caecilians.

children.name: Siphonops annulatus
children.description: Young scrape and eat layers of the mother's outer skin as their first food — a form of skin-feeding parental care described only in 2006; the mother's skin doubles in thickness in preparation.

children.name: Siphonops paulensis
children.description: A ringed caecilian of southern Brazil, Paraguay, Bolivia and northern Argentina. Like its relatives, mothers feed their young on a specialized layer of skin (dermatophagy).

children.name: Siphonops hardyi
children.description: A small ringed caecilian of southeastern Brazil's Atlantic Forest. Practices maternal skin-feeding of the young.

children.name: Siphonops insulanus
children.description: A ringed caecilian of Brazil, distinguished from the common ringed caecilian by subtle annulation differences; a soil dweller with maternal skin-feeding.

children.name: Dermophis
children.description: Central American caecilians, viviparous (live-bearing) burrowers of soil and leaf litter. The Mexican caecilian is the best-studied member and a model for caecilian reproduction.

children.name: Dermophis mexicanus
children.description: A live-bearing caecilian of Mexico and Central America that gestates young internally, feeding fetuses on uterine secretions. Found in agricultural soils and forests; listed as Vulnerable due to habitat loss.

children.name: Dermophis oaxacae
children.description: A burrowing caecilian of southwestern Mexico, named for the state of Oaxaca. A live-bearing species of moist lowland and montane soils.

children.name: Dermophis glandulosus
children.description: A live-bearing caecilian of Costa Rica, Panama and Colombia, with conspicuous skin glands. Found in lowland and foothill forest soils.

children.name: Gymnopis
children.description: Central American viviparous caecilians lacking eye openings as adults, burrowing in forest and plantation soils.

children.name: Gymnopis multiplicata
children.description: A live-bearing caecilian of Central America with a purplish-grey body and numerous body rings. Common in moist soils of forests, gardens and banana plantations from Honduras to Panama.

children.name: Geotrypetes
children.description: African soil caecilians, viviparous burrowers of West and Central African forests. The genus has become important in laboratory research on caecilian biology.

children.name: Geotrypetes seraphini
children.description: A live-bearing West and Central African caecilian, one of the few caecilians kept and bred in the lab. Pinkish-grey and soil-dwelling, it gives birth to fully developed young.

children.name: Schistometopum
children.description: Brightly colored African caecilians, including vivid yellow species of coastal East Africa and São Tomé. Their striking coloration is unusual among the typically drab caecilians.

children.name: Schistometopum thomense
children.description: A brilliant yellow caecilian endemic to the island of São Tomé in the Gulf of Guinea. Locally abundant in soils and plantations; one of the most colorful caecilians.

children.name: Schistometopum gregorii
children.description: A yellow-bodied caecilian of coastal Kenya, Tanzania and Mozambique, found in moist soils near rivers and floodplains.

children.name: Boulengerula
children.description: East African soil caecilians famous for maternal skin-feeding (dermatophagy): hatchlings peel and eat the lipid-rich outer skin of their mother. Named after the herpetologist George Boulenger.

children.name: Boulengerula taitanus
children.description: A Kenyan soil caecilian of the Taita Hills whose hatchlings feed on their mother's specially thickened, nutrient-rich skin, a behavior first documented in this species.

children.name: Boulengerula uluguruensis
children.description: A caecilian endemic to the Uluguru Mountains of Tanzania, restricted to montane forest soils and threatened by deforestation.

children.name: Boulengerula boulengeri
children.description: A soil caecilian of the Eastern Arc Mountains of Tanzania, an egg-laying species restricted to montane forest soils.

children.name: Herpele
children.description: Stout-bodied African forest caecilians of Central and West Africa, egg-laying soil burrowers with maternal skin-feeding behavior.

children.name: Herpele squalostoma
children.description: A robust caecilian of Central and West African rainforests whose young engage in maternal dermatophagy, feeding on the mother's skin. A widespread but secretive soil dweller.

children.name: Scolecomorphus
children.description: Tropical African caecilians remarkable for tentacles mounted near the nostrils and, in some, eyes carried on the tentacle that can be protruded from the head.

children.name: Scolecomorphus kirkii
children.description: An East African caecilian of Malawi, Tanzania and Mozambique notable for an eye attached to the base of its tentacle, which it can push out of the skull. A live-bearing soil dweller of montane forests.

children.name: Scolecomorphus vittatus
children.description: A striped caecilian of Tanzania's Eastern Arc and coastal forests, with protrusible tentacle-mounted eyes. Restricted to forest soils threatened by habitat loss.

children.name: Grandisonia
children.description: Caecilians endemic to the Seychelles islands, an ancient relict radiation isolated since the breakup of Gondwana.

children.name: Grandisonia alternans
children.description: A caecilian endemic to the granitic Seychelles, part of an ancient lineage isolated on the islands for tens of millions of years. Lives in moist forest and plantation soils.

children.name: Hypogeophis
children.description: Seychelles endemic caecilians, egg-laying soil burrowers that form a key part of the islands' relict amphibian fauna.

children.name: Hypogeophis rostratus
children.description: The most widespread Seychelles caecilian, found on several granitic islands. Females lay eggs in burrows and guard them until hatching into miniature adults (direct development).

children.name: Gegeneophis
children.description: Small soil caecilians endemic to peninsular India and the Western Ghats, egg-laying species with maternal care that are still being newly described.

children.name: Gegeneophis ramaswamii
children.description: A small soil caecilian of the southern Western Ghats, India, where mothers brood eggs and the young feed on the mother's skin. A model species for studying caecilian maternal care.

children.name: Gegeneophis carnosus
children.description: A pinkish soil caecilian endemic to the Western Ghats of India, an egg-laying species of moist forest and plantation soils.

children.name: Indotyphlus
children.description: Tiny soil caecilians endemic to the Western Ghats of India, among the smallest and least-known caecilians.

children.name: Indotyphlus battersbyi
children.description: A small caecilian endemic to the northern Western Ghats near Pune, India. Lives in lateritic soils and is rarely seen above ground.

children.name: Uraeotyphlus
children.description: Western Ghats caecilians intermediate between primitive and advanced families, egg-laying with an aquatic larval stage.

children.name: Uraeotyphlus oxyurus
children.description: A reddish-brown caecilian endemic to the Western Ghats of southern India, with a pointed tail. Lays eggs that develop through an aquatic larval stage in streams.

children.name: Rhinatrema
children.description: Primitive South American caecilians with a true tail and aquatic larvae, representing one of the most ancient caecilian lineages.

children.name: Rhinatrema bivittatum
children.description: A small striped caecilian of the Guianas and northern Brazil, one of the most primitive living caecilians, retaining a true tail. Lays eggs that hatch into aquatic larvae.

children.name: Epicrionops
children.description: Andean and Central American caecilians of an ancient family, tailed and egg-laying with free-living larvae.

children.name: Epicrionops bicolor
children.description: A primitive, tailed caecilian of the Andes of Ecuador and Peru, living in cool montane streams and soils. Retains larval features lost in more advanced caecilians.

children.name: Chthonerpeton
children.description: Aquatic and semiaquatic South American caecilians, live-bearing relatives of Typhlonectes found in rivers, swamps and wet soils.

children.name: Chthonerpeton indistinctum
children.description: A semiaquatic, live-bearing caecilian of southern Brazil, Uruguay and Argentina, found in marshes, ditches and slow waters. Gives birth to well-developed young.

children.name: Potomotyphlus
children.description: Fully aquatic South American caecilians of the Amazon basin, with a strongly compressed, eel-like body and finned tail for swimming.

children.name: Potomotyphlus kaupii
children.description: A wholly aquatic caecilian of the Amazon and Orinoco basins, with a laterally flattened, eel-like body adapted to permanent life in rivers. Live-bearing and rarely encountered.

children.name: Microcaecilia
children.description: Small Neotropical soil caecilians of the Guiana Shield and Amazonia, with many newly described species, including the one named after Donald Trump.

children.name: Microcaecilia dermatophaga
children.description: A small French Guianan caecilian in which hatchlings peel and consume the lipid-rich outer skin of their mother, the first New World caecilian shown to do so, reflected in its species name.

children.name: Microcaecilia unicolor
children.description: A uniformly colored small caecilian of the Guianas, a secretive soil-dweller of lowland rainforest.

children.name: Oscaecilia
children.description: Slender Neotropical caecilians with reduced eyes covered by bone, highly specialized soil burrowers of Central and South America.

children.name: Oscaecilia ochrocephala
children.description: A slender, pale-headed caecilian of Panama and Colombia with eyes covered by bone, an adaptation to a wholly subterranean life. Lays eggs in moist soil.
